coredata.dc:title: Zero-dimensional quantum dot-modified membrane materials for the effective treatment of industrial wastewater: A comprehensive review

coredata.dc:description: The efficient treatment and recycling of industrial wastewater is an important way to improve water resource utilization. Membrane separation technology has been widely used in the field of industrial wastewater treatment due to its high separation efficiency, low energy consumption, good scalability, and other advantages. A recent breakthrough in membrane technologies is the emergence of various nanocomposite membranes, which not only breaks the trade-off between membrane selectivity and permeability, but also enhances the anti-fouling ability and multi-scale separation effects of membranes. Among the nanomaterials used for membrane fabrication, zero-dimensional quantum dots (QDs) with size-dependent optical properties and excellent photocatalytic potential have become promising candidates for novel nanocomposite membrane construction. Significant improvements in membrane surface hydrophilicity, anti-fouling property, and permeability can be achieved by incorporating QDs during the membrane formation process or through membrane post-modification. This review focuses on recent advances in QD-modified membranes for industrial wastewater treatment. The synthesis methods of QDs were summarized firstly, especially for carbon-based and semiconductor QDs. Based on the blending modification and surface modification, the research progress in the modification strategies of QD-modified membranes is presented. Subsequently, the state-of-the-art studies of QD-modified membranes in the application of industrial wastewater treatment are reviewed. Finally, the current challenges and opportunities in QD-modified membranes are discussed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficient treatment and recycling of industrial wastewater is an important way to improve water resource utilization. Membrane separation technology has been widely used in the field of industrial wastewater treatment due to its high separation efficiency, low energy consumption, good scalability, and other advantages. A recent breakthrough in membrane technologies is the emergence of various nanocomposite membranes, which not only breaks the trade-off between membrane selectivity and permeability, but also enhances the anti-fouling ability and multi-scale separation effects of membranes. Among the nanomaterials used for membrane fabrication, zero-dimensional quantum dots (QDs) with size-dependent optical properties and excellent photocatalytic potential have become promising candidates for novel nanocomposite membrane construction. Significant improvements in membrane surface hydrophilicity, anti-fouling property, and permeability can be achieved by incorporating QDs during the membrane formation process or through membrane post-modification. This review focuses on recent advances in QD-modified membranes for industrial wastewater treatment. The synthesis methods of QDs were summarized firstly, especially for carbon-based and semiconductor QDs. Based on the blending modification and surface modification, the research progress in the modification strategies of QD-modified membranes is presented. Subsequently, the state-of-the-art studies of QD-modified membranes in the application of industrial wastewater treatment are reviewed. Finally, the current challenges and opportunities in QD-modified membranes are discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization, a large amount of industrial wastewater, characterized by high levels of refractory organic contaminants, high salinity, and high toxicity, has been discharged [1,2]. Thus, achieving efficient treatment and recycling of industrial wastewater has become an urgent global challenge. Compared to conventional evaporation and aerobic biochemical treatment technologies, membrane separation technology is an attractive method for achieving efficient treatment and recycling of industrial wastewater due to its high separation efficiency, low energy consumption, and convenient operation for large-scale production [3,4]. However, membrane separation technology also faces challenges, such as membrane fouling, the trade-off of membranes, and resource utilization of membrane filtration, which hinders their further development in industrial wastewater treatment [5].

To address these challenges, one of the breakthroughs in membrane separation research is the emergence of various composite membranes with tailored separation features by incorporating new materials during the membrane formation process or membrane post-modification [6,7]. These novel composite membranes demonstrate good performance in overcoming the trade-off between membrane selectivity and permeability, and solving the membrane fouling problem. With the gradual maturity of nanomaterial synthesis technology, two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets [8,9], one-dimensional (1D) nanotubes [10,11] and zero-dimensional (0D) nanoparticles [12,13] are widely used for membrane modification. Among them, 0D quantum dots (QDs), with a size range of 2 to 20 nm, have attracted significant research interest due to their excellent dispersibility, high photocatalytic performance, and uniform small size, which offers abundant active sites for regulating properties [14]. Some of semiconductor QDs, such as CdS, ZnS are often used as visible light photocatalysts to degrade pollutants by rejecting negatively charged dyes or bacterial impurities [15]. On the other hand, low toxic carbon-based QDs also show great potential in industrial wastewater treatment due to their high stability and good biocompatibility [16,17]. All in all, the incorporation of QDs into traditional membrane materials can effectively improve the hydrophilicity [18,19], permeability [20] and antifouling performance [21] of the pristine membranes. Furthermore, the larger surface to volume ratio of 0D QDs leads to their superior properties and good surface grating compared to 1D nanotubes and 2D nanosheets [22]. Compared to 2D GO nanosheets, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit enhanced peroxidase activity due to their excellent exceptional electron transport property, resulting in their higher catalytic activity [23]. Zeng et al. [21] also demonstrated that 0D graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) exhibit higher antibacterials and anti-biofouling than 1D nanotubes and 2D nanosheets during a continuous filtration test using E. coli. The study of Akmal’s group [24] indicated that the incorporation of CQDs increased the hydrophilicity of membranes more effectively than 2D GO sheets due to higher water flux and reverse salt flux of CQD-modified membranes. Fig. 1 a shows the development timeline of the QDs and the important advance in the synthetic technology.

To visualize current research trends in QD-modified membrane materials for industrial wastewater treatment, we mapped the literature from Web of Science in recent years by using “quantum dots”, “membrane” and “industrial wastewater treatment” as keywords. Fig. 1b shows the number of publications about QD-modified membranes and their application in the field of industrial wastewater treatment over the past ten years. The first paper on the use of QDs in membrane applications for industrial wastewater treatment was reported in 2014. The number of publications has increased exponentially since 2017. The rapidly increasing number of publications on QD-modified membranes for industrial wastewater treatment highlights the great potential of this emerging field. Although many research articles have extensively explored the performance of QD-modified membranes for industrial wastewater treatment, a systematic review on QD-modified membranes for industrial wastewater treatment has not been reported. There is still much work need to be devoted. For example, the treatment mechanism is still unclear due to the complex environment in industrial wastewater. There are many complex forms of oil in wastewater, such as emulsified oil, suspended oil, dispersed oil, etc. In addition, in the case of heavy metals, the separation, adsorption and degradation effects of membranes and QDs all play significant roles.

Therefore, this review focuses on recent advances of carbon-based QDs and semiconductor QDs for modification of membranes in industrial wastewater treatment. Considering that the optical properties of QDs are directly related to their synthesis methods, we first introduce the synthesis approaches of different types of QDs. We then summarize the different modification strategies of QD-modified membranes including mixed matrix membranes (MMMs), surface grafting membranes, surface coating membranes, as well as the latest developments in QD-modified 2D nanosheets-based membranes (2DNMs). Lastly, we discuss the recent advances of QD-modified membranes in the field of industrial wastewater treatment (including dye removal, heavy metal ion adsorption, oily wastewater separation and emerging contaminant removal) with the involved mechanism, and outline the prospects for their future development.

QDs are 0D nanomaterials with their size reduced to the nanometer scale in all three dimensions. They exhibit a discrete atom-like electronic structure and size-dependent energy levels due to the restricted electron motion. For example, CdSe QDs can exhibit full-spectrum luminescence from blue to red color range as their size changes. Thanks to their excellent photoelectric properties, QDs have been widely applied in light emitting diode (LEDs), solar cells, and biological imaging. The discovery of QDs can be traced back to 1983 with the successful synthesis of cadmium sulfide colloidal particles [25]. With the development of synthesis technology, low-toxic carbon QDs (CQDs) composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, were synthesized in 2004 [26]. Their controlled size and abundant active sites enable a wide tuning of chemical and physical functions. Researchers began to incorporate various QDs, such as CQDs, GQDs, and CdS QDs, into membrane synthesis to obtain membranes with improved performance. The size and morphology of QDs have a direct influence on the hydrophilicity and anti-fouling property of modified membranes, and the synthesis method is directly related to the size, morphology, and optical properties of QDs. Therefore, we first introduce the physical and chemical properties and synthesis methods of various types of QDs such as GQDs, GOQDs, CQDs, and semiconductor QDs, which have been reported for membrane separation.

Semiconductor QDs are 0D nanoparticles commonly composed of elements from groups IV, II-VI, IV-VI or III-V in the periodic table. By coating another inorganic material on the pristine core, core–shell QDs with improved stability and quantum yield are obtained [27]. In addition, alloyed QDs composed of three elements also have been prepared, such as CdSeS and ZnCdSe [28]. Besides, magic-sized QDs with specific atom number and structures also have been extensively studied in recent years [29]. Due to the quantum confinement effect, these QDs exhibit size-dependent emission (Fig. 2 a). In addition, their broadband extends across the visible and near-infrared spectra (Fig. 2b) [30]. The multi-exciton effect and large surface-to-volume ratio make them promising photocatalysts in wastewater treatment, photocatalytic hydrogen generation, and CO2 photoreduction.

Among semiconductor QDs, CdS QDs with a narrow bandgap (∼2.4 eV) and exceptional photocatalytic performance have been widely used in wastewater treatment [31,32]. The possible photocatalytic mechanism can be briefly described as: photogenerated electrons are firstly generated in the valence band and transferred to the conduction band of CdS QDs, as well as holes are generated in the valence band, forming the electron-hole pairs under light irradiation. After that, the redox reactions of electrons and holes occurs on the QD surface, resulting in reactive oxygen radicals such as peroxide radicals (•O2 −) and •OH radicals, which subsequently are utilized to degrade organic pollutants (Fig. 2c).

Synthetic strategies for semiconductor QDs can be mainly classified into physical vacuum-based methods and wet chemical approaches. Here we focused on the development of wet chemical approaches to semiconductor QDs. Pioneering work in synthesizing CdE (E=S, Se, Te) QDs in colloidal solutions by mixing Cd(CH3)2 and chalcogenide elements in trioctylphosphine (TOP) solvent [22] was reported in 1993. After that, Peng et al. [33] replaced the highly toxic, expensive and explosive Cd(CH3)2 to cheaper and less toxic CdO, which advanced the hot injection method in II-VI, III-V, and IV-VI semiconductor QDs. In their study, a mixture of CdO, trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO) and hexylphosphonic acid/tetradecylphosphonic acid was heated at 300 ℃ to generate a colorless solution, accompanied by the swiftly injecting of chalcogenide precursors to obtain high quality CdE (Te, Se, S) QDs. For the hot injection method, the nucleation and growth of QDs can be controlled by precursor reactivity, surface ligands, as well as the reaction temperature and duration, enabling precise control over the stoichiometry, size, and shape of the QDs. Subsequently, they changed the coordination solvent TOPO to the non-coordination solvent octadecene (ODE), preparing high-quality CdS QDs successfully [34]. After that, ODE was used as the most common solvent in the synthesis system of QDs. However, the complex operation steps limit the large-scale production via the hot injection method. Therefore, non-injectable strategies were developed in 2004. Cao group [35] reported the synthesis of high-quality CdS QDs by adding the Cd and S source, myristic acid, and ODE in one-pot. For this case, CdS QDs were capped by organic molecules, which should be replaced by hydrophilic ligands for further wastewater treatment. Therefore, the direct synthesis of QDs in aqueous phase is beneficial for their applicaiton in wastewater treatment. A study [36] reported the design of CdS @ polysulfone (PSF) membrane for wastewater remediation system. The prepared CdS @ PSF membrane significantly enhanced the degradation rate of dyes under alkaline conditions. In 2016, Canan Baslak et al. [37] first mixed CdCl2·5/2H2O and 3-mercaptopropinoic acid (MPA) to prepare the Cd(MPA)2 precursor. After that, NaHSe and NaHTe aqueous solutions were injected at 100 ℃ to synthesize CdTeSe QDs in aqueous phase. Finally, a novel polymer inclusion membrane with CdTeSe QDs as the carrier was prepared to promote the transport of rhodamine B (RB). The maximum transport of RB was achieved at pH of 12.

Overall, from the colloidal chemistry point of view, the synthetic technique of QDs has reached its maturity and it can now be used to produce nearly monodisperse QDs. The next advancement in atomically precise synthesis of QDs with desired stoichiometry, size, shape, and structure requires the in-depth understanding of the nucleation of QDs.

Carbon-based QDs are quasi or quasi-spherical 0D conductive nanoparticles composed of nontoxic carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen. They have rapidly become a research hotpot due to their green, low-toxic, superior sustainable, and biocompatible nature [38]. Carbon-based QDs exhibit novel physical and chemical properties compared to 2D nanosheets and 1D nanobelts due to stronger quantum confinement and edge effects at particle sizes below 10 nm. As a result, these materials exhibit excellent photo-luminescent, antibacterial, photo-induced electron transfer, and semiconductor properties. These properties make them suitable for various fields, such as heavy metal ions adsorption, removal of organic and inorganic pollutants, and the photocatalytic degradation of wastewater pollutants. The presence of abundant active sites and functional groups on the surface of carbon-based QDs enhances their biocompatibility, water solubility, and photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The inherent photocatalytic properties of carbon-based QDs can be used to develop self-cleaning and self-regenerating membranes to degrade contaminants under light irradiation, ensuring the long-term use of membranes.

Common types of carbon-based QDs mainly include CQDs, GQDs, GOQDs, graphitic carbon nitride QDs (g-CNQDs). At present, there is no universal nomenclature for carbon-based QDs, so we established a generalized nomenclature according to Sevde Korkut et al. [38] and Guan et al. [39]. When fluorescent nanocarbons exhibit quantum confinement and crystal structure, they are divided into CQDs or GQDs according to their shape. CQDs are composed of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon, while GQDs are composed of sp2 hybridized graphite cores and carbonyl surface. GOQDs are nanoscale fragments derived from graphene oxide (GO) sheets with lateral sizes typically less than 100 nm and more functional groups. g-CNQDs are obtained from the reduction of bulk graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to 10 nm [39]. g-C3N4 is a polymer semiconductor composed of carbon and nitrogen in sp2 hybridization on the π-conjugated graphite plane and has a narrower band gap [38]. In 2011, g-CNQDs were first prepared [40] with excellent water solubility and photoluminescence based on a simple heat treatment strategy. Since then, 0D g-CNQDs have been extensively investigated [38].

The synthesis methods of carbon-based QDs are classified into “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods (Fig. 3 ). Top-down methods involve a series of crushing treatments to the break bulk material into 0D QDs, while bottom-up methods are based on the self-assembly and polymerization of small molecule precursors to generate macromolecular intermediates, which are then decomposed and carbonized to form 0D QDs. In this review, we introduce several common synthesis methods in detail.

In 2004, Scrivens et al. [26] first discovered fluorescent carbon nanoparticles while experimenting with the arc discharge method to extract and purify the crude product of carbon nanotubes from carbon ash with HNO3 solution. These particles exhibited yellow orange color with a quantum yield of 0.016 under 365 nm UV light and were later named as CQDs. However, this method is not suitable for industrial production due to their inhomogeneous particle size, excessive impurities, and low yield. Laser ablation is another method for preparing carbon-based QDs by irradiating a carbon source with a laser in an inert range. In 2006, Sun et al. pioneered the synthesis of CQDs with precise control of size and morphology through laser ablation [41]. They pressed a mixture of graphite powder into carbon targets, laser ablated them at 900 °C and 75 kPa pressure, and obtained CQDs with high optical emission at 5 nm after passivation. In 2008, Hu et al. [42] utilized graphite as a carbon source and polyethylene glycol as a passivator to successfully prepare CQDs after irradiating the passivator solution with a laser. Compared to the previous methods, the size of the obtained CQDs is smaller, and it was found that the luminescence of CQDs could be controlled by changing the type of organic solvent. Unlike arc discharge, the major advantage of this method is that it keeps the original raw materials without using dangerous chemicals, but continuous stirring during the reaction is required to avoid precipitation. On the other hand, QDs synthesized by electrochemical oxidation offer several advantages such as being conducted at room temperature and ambient pressure, producing QDs with high purity, high yield, controllable shape, and reproducible results [39]. The electrochemical pathway usually utilizes a precursor with sp2-carbon domain structures, which is beneficial to control the size of the QDs. Zhang et al. utilized graphite rod, Pt foil and NaOH solution as an anode, counter electrode, and electrolyte, respectively, to synthesize uniformly sized GQDs with strong yellow emission with a quantum yield of 14 % at a current intensity in the range of 80 ∼ 200 mA·cm−2. The obtained GQDs are single-layer graphene with a diameter of 5 ∼ 10 nm and a thickness of less than 0.5 nm. In a recent study, Kalita et al. [43] proposed electrochemical synthesis of GQDs with an adjustable size by using GO and LiClO4 as electrodes and electrolytes in propylene carbonate, respectively.

The hydrothermal method offers an advantage due to the availability of widely accessible precursors, including various low-cost and eco-friendly substances. QDs synthesized by this method are extensively used in membrane modification. Jia et al. [44] reported a one-pot hydrothermal method for the synthesis of CQDs at low temperature. They dissolved vitamin C and copper acetate in water, stirred the mixture for 5 h at 90 °C, followed by centrifugation and purification to obtain CQDs. Gu et al. [45] heated CA at 200 ℃ for 30 min to form GOQDs with an obvious color change to orange. The obtained GOQDs with an average size of about 3.8 nm were grafted onto the surface of a ceramic membrane to enhance its water permeability and antifouling properties. In 2014, Wang et al. [46] introduced a multi-step hydrothermal method. They first etched 3D bulk g-C3N4 into 2D nanosheets by thermal oxidation, then further etched with H2SO4 and HNO3 to obtain 1D nanobelts, and finally hydrothermally treated at 200 °C to obtain 0D QDs with size of 5 ∼ 9 nm. Although the preparation process is complex, the obtained QDs exhibit good crystal structure, water solubility, and up-conversion characteristics. This multi-step method is also widely used in the synthesis of g-C3N4 and its composites. The microwave-assisted method offers the advantages of heating the carbon precursor more convenient and faster. CQDs can be obtained in a few minutes, and the size is more uniform. In 2009, Zhu et al. [47] pioneered the synthesis of CQDs via more convenient and faster microwave synthesis. They microwave-heated the mixture of glucose and polyethylene glycol to prepare CQDs at different durations. Interestingly, when heated for 5 min, CQDs with a particle size of 2.75 ± 0.45 nm and a quantum yield of 6.3 % were obtained. When heated for 10 min, CQDs with a particle size of 3.65 ± 0.6 nm and a quantum yield of 3.1 % were obtained. The time of microwave heating significantly affected the particle size and fluorescence characteristics of CQDs. Yuan et al. [48] prepared uniformly dispersed CDs with sizes of 2 ∼ 5 nm by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of critic acid (CA) within five minutes, and subsequently prepared composite membrane by impregnating CDs on polyacrylonitrile carriers via interfacial polymerization (IP), which greatly improved the permeation and the hydrophilic properties of the membrane. Recently, Shi et al. [49] synthesized GQDs with a uniform size via microwave-assisted one-step pyrolysis of CA and diethylenetriamine in a short time, integrating them into Pebax, a poly(ether-b-amide) copolymer, to fabricate molecular-scale mixed matrix membrane with better hydrophilicity. However, the high energy consumption of microwave heating may limit its suitability for large-scale production. Aside from the commonly used hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods, advanced synthetic technologies such as ultrasonic, magnetic hyperthermia and microfluidic methods have been developed in recent years. For instance, Chen et al. [50] developed a magnetic hyperthermia method for the synthesis of CDs, which overcomes traditional heat transfer limitations and is expected to achieve the large-scale industrial production of QDs. The cost-effective ultrasonic method also has been used by Zehira for the synthesis of highly photocatalytic SnO2 QDs without using high-temperature and high-pressure equipment [51]. The microfluidic method is another choice to achieve the large-scale industrial production of QDs, which was successfully utilized to prepare perovskite QDs by precisely controlling reaction parameters [52]. Table 1 summarizes the synthesis techniques and surface function of carbon-based QDs and semiconductor QDs as well as their modification methods of different membranes.

In summary, QDs of different types and synthesis methods play different roles in enhancing membrane performance. The unique size of QDs enables them to be uniformly dispersed in the membrane matrix and on the membrane surface. When dealing with various types of industrial wastewater, QDs are conducive to resisting the membrane pollution caused by high concentrations and diverse pollutants, and they can enhance membrane separation ability through photocatalysis, adsorption, and other effects.

Common methods of membrane modification include surface coating [82], chemical treatment, heat treatment and solvent treatment [83]. In this section, we will introduce the QD-modified membranes using various modification methods, such as mixing matrix, surface grafting, and surface coating. In addition, we will summarize the latest developments in QD-modified 2DNMs.

MMMs are usually fabricated by nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS), which involves mixing linking agents and membrane-forming materials to prepare a casting solution that eventually forms a membrane through phase inversion. NIPS effectively regulates the final morphology and pore structure of membranes to enhance membrane performance by controlling the loadings of QDs.

Zhao et al. [19] reported the preparation of GOQD-modified PSF ultrafiltration (UF) membrane with various GOQD loadings via NIPS (Fig. 4 a). The enhanced hydrophilicity of the prepared membrane was attributed to the increased amounts of functional groups of small-sized GOQDs (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the membrane incorporated with 0.1 and 0.3 wt% of GOQDs exhibited wider finger-like micro-voids compared to the pristine PSF membrane, resulting in increased mass transfer, and improved antifouling performance of the pristine membrane. Recently, external magnetic and electric fields have been used as assisted factors for mixed matrix membranes construction [84–87]. Zhang et al. [71] fabricated a CQD-incorporated polyethersulfone (PES) membrane with the assistance of direct current (DC) electric field (Fig. 4c). The electric field induced a rearrangement of PES molecular chains in the casting solution and accelerated the migration of hydrophilic CQDs to the top surface of the membrane, resulting in a higher porosity of the prepared membrane surface. The DC electric field also promoted the enrichment of CQDs on the membrane surface. The highest pure water permeability was achieved as 478.2 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 when the loading of CQDs was 0.5 wt%. At this concentration, the solvent recovery rate and the BSA removal rate were obtained as 95.3 % and 98 %, respectively.

The influence of QDs in the casting solution on the thermodynamics and kinetics of membrane formation has also been investigated. One study [18] utilized hydrophilic 2-mercaptoethanol to encapsulate ZnS QDs to prepare a ZnS QD-embedded PES membrane by NIPS. Encapsulation of ZnS QDs was achieved by adding 2-mercaptoethanol to the precursor solution. The addition of small amount of 2-mercaptoethanol-encapsulated ZnS QDs increased the solvent/nonsolvent exchange rate and thermodynamic instability, thereby accelerating the phase inversion process. This improvement in the thermodynamic and kinetic properties of the phase inversion process resulted in a denser surface layer and a more porous sublayer, improving the hydrophilicity of the membrane. Furthermore, it was demonstrated that large amounts of QDs may agglomerate and increase the viscosity of the casting solution, hindering the pore and thin layer formation. This finding aligns with previous report by Zhao et al. [19], where an increase in QDs content led to increased porosity and pore size of the membrane.

Doping metal ions in QDs is an efficient method for regulating the properties of QDs, making metal-doped QDs promising candidate for membrane modification with superior properties. Vatanpour’s group studied the influence of metal-doped ZnS QDs on membrane properties [81]. The Mn2+, Fe2+ and Co2+ cation-doped ZnS QDs were firstly synthesized via a chemical precipitation method and incorporated to PSF membrane to form nanocomposite membrane using NIPS. Compared with pristine membrane and metal-free ZnS QDs, metal-doped ZnS QDs resulted in membrane with higher porosity and wider pore structure than the pristine membrane (Fig. 4d), which exhibited a superior performance in terms of hydrophilicity, antifouling performance, and dye rejection. Specifically, the highest pure water flux and BSA flux were achieved with the membrane with 1 % Fe-doped ZnS QDs.

Surface grafting is another approach for modifying membranes with QDs. This method involves covalently binding end-functionalized polymers bearing appropriate reactive groups to the membranes surface, enabling the attachment of surface-modified polymers or large molecular adhesives. This method can effectively improve the membrane's hydrophilicity and surface roughness.

Li et al. [20] reported the grafting of amino-functionalized GOQDs (NH2-GOQDs) onto the surface of a composite film through an amide coupling reaction (Fig. 5 a). The resulting membrane surface exhibited a “leaf-like” structure when NH2-GOQDs were embedded in the polyamide (PA) layer. Furthermore, they demonstrated that the surface roughness of the membrane was increased initially and then decreased with increasing NH2-GOQDs contents, and the highest value of the surface roughness was obtained as 83.6 nm. In addition, the water contact angle significantly decreased when NH2-GOQDs were grafted onto the membrane, indicating a significant enhancement in hydrophilicity. Gu et al. [45] pioneered the grafting of GOQDs on ceramic Al2O3 membrane (Al2O3-GOQDs) by introducing (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) as a coupling agent. APTES facilitated the attachment of GOQDs to the ceramic membrane by forming an amide bond between its –NH2 group and –COOH group of GOODs (Fig. 5b). This modification led to a color change in the membrane from white to light-brown, indicating successful embedding of GOQDs on the membrane surface. Atomic force microscope topography scan images revealed that the surface roughness of the grafted membrane reduced compared to the pristine membrane, suggesting the increased antifouling ability of the membrane. At the same time, the hydrophilicity of the membrane was also slightly improved when GOQDs were grafted on the surface of the membrane. The GOQDs functionalized polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane was also been studied by Zeng and co-workers [21]. In this work, 3-aminopropyl-trimethoxysilane was added to linkage between GOQDs and PVDF membrane (Fig. 5c). As the GOQDs concentration further increased, multi-layer GOQDs were covered on the membrane surface, which increased the resistance of water molecules through the membrane in the grafting modification. As a result, the pure water permeability coefficient of GOQDs modified PVDF membrane was slightly lower than that of the pristine membrane. To address the reduced water permeability issue, Yi et al. [72] prepared a nitrogen-doped GOQDs (N-GOQDs)-grafted PA membrane by surface grafting modification (Fig. 5d). The numerous –NH2 and polar groups on the N-GOQDs facilitated water flux enlargement by offsetting the resistance of the graft layer and facilitating the passage of more water molecules through the membrane.

In addition to surface grafting modification, coating is another surface modification method where QDs are simply adsorbed onto the membrane surface or in situ solidified on the membrane surface through secondary interactions, providing enhanced stability.

Zhao et al. [73] pioneered the use of GOQDs as nano-coatings to improve the filtration efficiency of thin-film composite PA nanofiltration (NF) membrane by pressure-assisted filtration method (Fig. 6 a). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) images revealed complete coverage of the membrane surface by GOQDs, resulting in increased water permeability and enhanced hydrophilicity. The rich hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in GOQDs also increased the hydrophilicity of the coated membrane. The dye separation and antifouling performance of the GOQDs coated membranes were also improved. Considering the aggregation of charged salts and organic molecules on the surface of the coated membranes leads to a decreased flux during the separation process, Shao et al. [75] designed an ordered polydopamine (PDA)-CQDs coating strategy. In this approach, PES served as the substrate, with PDA and CQDs sequentially coated onto the surface. A thin PA film was then formed on top of the membrane surface, resulting in a “PDA-CQDs-thin film composite (TFC)” membrane (Fig. 6b). The incorporation of CQDs as an intercalation layer not only enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the coated membrane, but also provided more hydrophilic water transport channels, resulting in enhanced permeability. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis indicated a higher internal spacing in the PDA-CQDs-TFC compared to pristine TFC and CQDs-TFC, facilitating faster water transport.

In addition to surface coating, IP is another common method based on dip-coating modification [88]. This method allows QDs to penetrate into the pores of the membrane to form a coating, which is more stable and uniform. Yeh’s group [76] prepared NGQDs-PA membrane by using piperazine and trimesoylchloride as polymer precursors, with NGQDs as additives. The SEM images showed that the NGQDs-PA membrane had more voids and the increased interlayer spacing compared to the pristine membrane. Theoretical calculations supported these findings, showing an increment in voids as NGQDs content increased from 0 to 1.0 %. This resulted in larger pores and a wider pore size distribution. At the same time, Mi et al. [74] achieved the simultaneous regulation of membrane surface charge and pore size by adjusting the diffusion of CQDs. Lysine pyrolysis generated L-CQDs, which were added to NF-CQDs membrane via IP (Fig. 6c). Zeta potential analysis revealed a shift in surface charge from positive to negative as the concentration of L-CQDs increased from 0.1 to 1.0 wt%. This concentration range also induced changes in pore size, with lower concentrations resulting in a looser separation layer and larger pore size (Fig. 6d). This control over membrane surface charge and pore size led to higher separation and antifouling performance of NF-CQDs membrane. In addition, high rejection rates of MgCl2, CuCl2 and CaCl2 (Fig. 6e) indicated great potential for water softening.

In recent years, 2DNMs have emerged as a novel branch of separation membranes [89]. These membranes are fabricated by stacking various 2D nanosheets such as GO [90,91], MXene [92,93], g-C3N4, 2D metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [94], and 2D covalent organic frameworks (COFs) to construct precise interlayer separation channels.

Xiong et al. [78] designed a super-permeable sulfonated carbon QDs (SCDs)/GO composite membrane. The amino groups on the surface of SCDs formed strong covalent bonds with the epoxy groups on the GO nanosheets (Fig. 7 a), ensuring the stable structure of the GO interlayer channels in the longitudinal direction and achieving simultaneous improvement in separation performance and long-term stability. In addition, sulfonic acid and amino groups functionalized CQDs (FQDs) were also introduced into the GO membranes (Fig. 7b) [77]. The cross-linking interaction between GO nanosheets and FQDs by hydrogen bond, effectively inhibiting membrane swelling and enhancing structural stability. The separation performance also remained stable within 144 h.

Yang et al. [95] synthesized a novel CdS QDs/Ti3C2Tx photocatalyst to increase the photocatalytic performance of CdS QDs (Fig. 7c). Ti3C2Tx MXene not only accelerated the charge carrier transfer, but also provided suitable attachment sites for CdS QDs. They showed that the degradation efficiency of prepared composites for the carbamazepine could be tuned by the deposition amount of CdS QDs. Furthermore, the membrane exhibited excellent degradation efficiency for emerging organic contaminants, such as deionized secondary water, effluent of municipal wastewater and groundwater.

For emerging 2DNMs, high flux under continuous operation could lead to serious membrane fouling while photo-assisted membrane catalytic cleaning is an effective method to solve the pollution problem. Li et al. [80] added CdS QDs into the CTF-1 framework to prepare an in-situ clean composite membrane (Fig. 7d). CdS QDs extended the length of charge-transfer, promoting the improvement of electron-hole separation efficiency. Additionally, the synergistic effect of CdS QDs and CTF-1 generated a high concentration of active free radicals. These factors made the CdS/CTF-1 have superior photocatalytic in-situ cleaning performance. Furthermore, CdS QDs increased the inter-nanosheet spacing, enhancing mass transfer channels. As a result, CdS CTF-1exhibited high water flux of > 170 L·m−2·h−1 and high dye removal rate of > 94 %. Under sunlight irradiation, the contaminated CdS/CTF-1 membrane could be regenerated with permeability restoration of 95 % in multiple cycles. Cao et al. [79] in situ grew CdS QDs on g-C3N4 nanosheets by a solvent thermal method for light-driven photocatalytic hydrogen generation. The aggregation of CdS QDs was effectively inhibited by g-C3N4 nanosheets through physisorption of CdS QDs and their oxygen-containing defects and amino groups to immobilize CdS QDs. When 12 wt% CdS QDs were loaded on g-C3N4 nanosheets, the CdS/g-C3N4 nanocomposite membrane showed a hydrogen generation rate of 4.494 mmol·h−1·g−1, which was 115 times than pure g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QD-modified membranes for industrial wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para: QD-modified membranes hold tremendous promise for industrial wastewater treatment due to their ability to overcome the trade-off between membrane selectivity and permeability while also improving anti-fouling ability and enabling multi-scale separation. These advantages make them highly effective for removing various contaminants from wastewater streams, ensuring cleaner water and safer industrial processes. Table 2 summarizes the application of QD-modified membranes for different types pollutants in industrial wastewater treatment.

The traditional dye treatment techniques, such as adsorption, advanced oxidation and microbial degradation, still face low efficiency and poor feasibility. The emerging of QD-modified membranes offers significant advantages in the degradation of dyes in industrial wastewater. Based on molecular sieving or the Donnan effect, the high rejection rates for dye molecules of QD-modified membranes effectively improves their selectivity. For instance, Zhang et al. [69] incorporated GOQDs into tannic acid (TA) layer of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane to prepared TA/GOQDs thin-film with enhanced dye rejection. The TA/GOQDs TFN membrane had a pure water flux of 23.33 L·m−2·h−1 at 0.2 MPa, and its dye rejection rates for Congo red and methyl blue reached to 99.8 % and 97.6 %, respectively. This was attributed to the negative charge of the membrane surface after GOQDs incorporation, which is responsible for their higher rejection for negatively charged dye molecules (Congo red, methyl blue (MB) and methyl orange). Gan et al. [96] prepared a CQDs/Ag/PSF membrane via NIPS, which was used to filter tartrazine dye with high water flux by size isolation. The pore size of CQDs/Ag/PSF membrane closely depended on the loading of Ag/CQDs. The highest permeability of composite membrane was reached to 64.75 ± 3.27 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 when the content of Ag/CQDs was 0.5 wt%. Further increasing the content of Ag/CQDs, the pore size of the membrane switched to reducing because the high viscosity of Ag/CQDs slowed down the phase transformation process.

By coupling with 2DNMs such as MOFs, COFs and GO, the photocatalytic property of QDs can be obviously improved, which is benefited to their further application in dye degradation. For example, Zheng et al. [97] utilized CQDs coupled with MIL-88A to enhance the photocatalytic effect. In a prepared MIL-88A/CQDs membrane, the incorporation of CQDs accelerated the electron transport of MIL-88A and improved the removal efficiency of dye by photo-Fenton reaction. After several cycles, the removal rate of MB and humic acid could be maintained more than 80 % and 90 %, respectively. Furthermore, the contaminated membrane could achieve self-cleaning in 20 min. They proposed that the separation process of e− and h+ pairs as well as the formation of •OH were accelerated by CQDs. As a result, the pollutants degrading process was accelerated by photo-Fenton catalysis. By replacing of another MOF material (UiO-66-NH2), the designed UiO-66-NH2/CQDs membrane [94] showed additional size-sieving capability for dyes of lower molecular weights (methyl orange: 96.10 %; methylene blue: 99.66 %).

Besides carbon-based QDs, other types QDs also are introduced for enhancing photocatalytic degradation efficiency of dyes. Alsohaimi et al. [36] embedded CdS QDs into PSF to prepare a photoactive membrane for effective removal of MB. Their photocatalytic degradation efficiency for MB enhanced with the increase of CdS QDs loadings. When the content of CdS QDs was 0.5 wt%, the removal rate was high up 79 %. They attributed it to the accessibility of more active sites of CdS that were available for MB degradation. On the other hand, there was a high removal rate at alkaline condition, which resulted from generation of •OH radicals caused by the abundance of hydroxide ions. Interestingly, Liu et al. [98] pointed that acidic conditions were benefited for the degradation of RB. Under alkaline conditions, electrostatic repulsion between RB and the catalyst was caused by the dissociation of aromatic carboxylic acid groups on RB and the deprotonation of nitrogen-containing groups, resulting in decreased adsorption capacity. In the Bi2MoO6/g-CNQDs/SiO2 composite membranes, Bi2MoO6 worked as photocatalyst to generate photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which directly degraded RB. g-CNQDs accelerated the electron transfer and reduced oxygen to superoxide radicals, further promoting the degradation of RB (Fig. 8 a).

By introducing QDs nanomaterial, QD-modified membranes hold more activity sites to absorb heavy metal ions. Song et al. [105] grafted 0D quaternized CQDs (QCDs) onto NF membrane for removing heavy metals and pharmaceutically active compounds in contaminated water. The rejection rate of target heavy metals showed that the electrostatic interaction plays a key role. The pore size of QCDs-TFC membrane was slightly decreased, and the rejection selectivity was further increased after introducing QDs. The positively charged of QCD-TFC membrane surface led to a high removal rate of 98.9 % and high permeability of 20.8 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 for metal cations. Furthermore, the introducing of zwitterions modified CQDs (ZQDs), as demonstrated by Yang et al. [106], enhanced the hydrophilicity and antifouling properties of membranes for Cu2+ removal. Compare to tertiary amine CQDs (TQDs) and CQDs, the modified membrane with ZQDs showed a higher Cu2+ rejection rate of 95 % and pure water flux of 6227.4 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. The superior membrane performance of ZQDs incorporated membrane is attributed to the increased surface porosity and roughness, as evidenced by SEM images. On the other hand, the strong attraction of zwitterions to water molecules resulted in higher hydrophilicity and antifouling properties through electrostatic interaction.

In 2021, Pandya et al. [104] reported the fabrication of an ordered multilayer GQD-embedded bacterial cellulose nanopaper (BCN) filtration membrane module, which exhibited a good performance for heavy metal ions removal in industrial scale. When the flow rate of industrial dye wastewater was 20 mL/min, the removal rate of Hg2+ and Pb2+ was as high as 99.6 % and 97 %, respectively. The module exhibited significant reductions in the standard aqueous concentrations of Hg2+ and Pb2+ even after multiple cycles, highlighting its robust membrane regeneration ability. In addition, Wang et al. [107] studied the absorption performance of N-GQDs hybrid membranes (NHMs) in a mixed polymetallic solution. The negatively charged membranes by introducing N-GQDs showed high sorption capacity for Cu2+ (9.03 mg/g) and Co2+(2.17 mg/g) due to electrostatic interaction. By introducing different functional groups, making NHMs exhibited different adsorption behaviors for Cu2+, Co2+ and Pb2+. Therefore, NHMs can selectively remove Cu2+, Co2+ and Pb2+ in mixed polymetallic solution. The involved mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 8b.

Membrane separation technology has been regarded as an effective method for efficient oil–water separation. Lei et al. [114] prepared hydrophobic C18-CQDs by grafting CQDs with octadecylamine, which reacted with •OH of membrane surface to form C18-CQDs membrane through covalently connecting. The consuming of •OH caused superhydrophobic and superoleophilic performance of C18-CQDs membrane. The permeability performance of the C18-CQDs membrane had been quantitatively evaluated, which exhibited a flux of ˃145 kg·m−2·h−1 for water vapor and 600 L·m−2·min−1 for various oil. The fourier transform infrared spectroscopy study demonstrated that C18-CQDs membrane showed high separation efficiencies of 99 % for water/hexane and water/dodecane. As illustrated in Fig. 8c, the oil was removed and saltwater was repelled when oil-saltwater mixtures were introduced onto the membrane. C18-CQDs membrane realized simultaneous oil–water separation and saltwater desalination. By introducing a mixture of β-FeOOH nanorods and CQDs, a BM@(β-FeOOH/CQDs) membrane [115] with superior fouling resistance was designed. The separation efficiency was more than 99 % for various oil–water emulsions. The adding of CQDs increased the loading yield of β-FeOOH nanorods on the membrane, which was benefited to activate the photo-Fenton reaction. As a result, BM@β-FeOOH/CQDs membrane could photo-activate H2O2 and generate •OH radicals to degrade oil droplets on the surface.

In addition, QDs could been prepared to coatings and then applied on textile fabric to enhance the stability of composite membranes for oil/water separation [116]. The absorption rate and superhydrophobic property of the membrane remained constant after ten cycles test. The CQD-based superhydrophobic membrane exhibited excellent separation capacity for petroleum ether, n-hexane, and silicone oil. Recently, ZQD also has been used with cellulose acetate tubular membrane in oil–water separation [117]. The rejection rate of ZQD/cellulose acetate tubular membrane for various oil samples was greater than 94 %. The quaternary amine and sulfonate groups of ZQD led to a higher hydrophilicity of ZQD/cellulose acetate tubular membrane, reducing the adhesion of oil molecules on the surface.

Recent years, the emerge of emerging contaminants such as persistent organic pollutants (POPs), endocrine disrupting chemicals, antibiotics and microplastics has attracted numerous attentions. Deng et al. [120] reported the preparation Ag/N-GQDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites for degrading tetracycline (TC). They first prepared N-GQDs/g-C3N4 through NIPS, and then prepared Ag/N-GQDs/g-C3N4 composite by photoreduction procedure. With the assisting of N-GQDs, the ternary nanocomposites exhibited full-spectrum light response ability, even in near-infrared light. The synergistic effect among Ag, N-GQDs, and g-C3N4 boosted the photocatalytic activity with a high removal efficiency of 92.8 % under full-spectrum light. The Ag nanoparticles (NPs) and N-GQDs transferred and reserved electrons, which accelerated the migration of photoinduced electrons, inhibiting the recombination (Fig. 8d). It is worthy to note that excess Ag NPs and N-GQDs tend to be a recombination center, causing a decreased photocatalytic performance. While research on QDs composite membranes for the removal of emerging contaminants is relatively limited, studies like the one by Deng et al. demonstrate the potential of QD-modified membranes for effectively addressing these contaminants. Further research in this area could lead to the development of advanced membrane technologies and materials for the removal of emerging contaminants from wastewater.

The main removal mechanism of QD-modified membranes in the industrial wastewater can be classified into: adsorption, electrostatic repulsion, sieving or photocatalysis. In the case of dye removal and heavy metal adsorption, the abundant surface areas and functional groups of QDs can effectively enhance the affinity to dye molecules and heavy metal ions, leading to high adsorption efficiencies. For instance, the function groups (C-O, –COOH, –NH2 and –OH) of NGQDs increased the adsorption capacity of poly(vinyl alcohol) membrane for Ag+ to 317.35 mg/g [121]. In addition, the introducing of QDs with different charge into membranes can realize the selective adsorption of dyes and heavy metal ions by electrostatic interaction. For example, the introducing of GOQD caused a more negatively charging of the TFC-PA-NF membrane surface, which increased the water flux of methyl orange and dye rejection of methylene blue due to the electrostatic repulsion and electrostatic attraction, respectively [69]. The added QDs can adjust the pore size and superhydrophilicity or superhydrophobicity of membrane when the QD-modified membranes are applied to the oily wastewater separation. By reacting with •OH of membrane surface, the oil–water separation efficiency of C18-CQDs membrane was significantly improved when the addition of C18-CQDs with isocyanate groups [114]. Besides, the adsorption, sieving and charge repulsion play a synergistic role in the removal of emerging contaminants.

Novel composite membranes based on nanomaterials have attracted much attention of researchers due to their effective improvement of membrane permeability, selectivity, and separation efficiency. Among these materials, 0D QDs can effectively break the trade-off between membrane selectivity and permeability while improving membrane hydrophilicity and anti-fouling property owing to their unique quantum confinement effects and abundant active sites. By matrix mixing, surface grafting and surface coating, the prepared QD-modified membranes show great promising in the treatment of industrial wastewater, including dye removal, heavy metal adsorption, oily wastewater separation and emerging contaminants removal. The hydrophilicity, permeability, separation performance and treatment mechanism of QD-modified membranes have been widely studied by several groups. We hope our review on QD-modified membrane materials for industrial wastewater treatment will give a comprehensive overview for the interested readers.

Despite their great potential, the research on QD-modified membrane materials for industrial wastewater treatment is still in its infancy, and several challenges need to be addressed:
1.
Given the size and size distribution of QDs have direct influence on the permeability, hydrophilicity and separation performance of QD-modified membrane materials, more precisely controllable synthetic technologies of QDs are urgently needed, especially for industrial large-scale production. On the other hand, the cost-effectiveness of QDs is also need to be considered in the large-scale production. The researchers are still pursuing economic synthesis methods and novel starting materials with more cost-effective. Additionally, the lifecycle impacts and toxicity of QDs during the practical application should be valued and further explored.
2.
Ensuring the stability of QDs during membrane separation processes is crucial for their photocatalytic performance. QDs are prone to degradation and instability in harsh environments, so encapsulating QDs with inert materials has been explored to improve their stability. However, this strategy may limit the charge transfer ability of QDs. Developing of new synthesis methods to directly prepare stable QDs materials without sacrificing charge transfer ability is essential.
3.
The overall stability of QD-modified membranes is also important for the membrane performance. In terms of MMMs, QDs inevitably lose from the membranes after long-term penetration. Thus, affinity between QDs and polymer matrix is a key factor to stabilize QDs on the membranes. In the case of 2DNMs, the van der Waals force of adjacent nanosheets is easily weaken when 2DNMs are immersed in the liquid phase, which enlarges the interlayer spacing and even destroys the membrane structure. Introducing QDs grafted or coated with small molecules (such as PDA or TA) is suggested to enhance the interfacial adhesion between QDs and nanosheets.
4.
Although the mechanism between QD-modified membrane and pollutants has been investigated, the in-depth understanding under complex pollution conditions is lacking. Future research is needed to study the mutual influence of QD-modified membranes on different types of pollutants in mixed pollution conditions. The presence of modeling approaches including molecular dynamic simulations (MDS) and artificial intelligence (AI) approaches, contributes to further application in membrane-based water treatment. MDS has been used to reveal the adhesion ability of different pollutants on the surface of QD-modified membranes. The mechanism of membranes catalysis and adsorption are revealed by the first principal density functional theory. In addition, AI can give the guideline for the design and construction of multiple antifouling QD-modified membranes. Hybridization of various compute tools is recommended for extending the application scope of QD-modified membranes to cover water resource management.
5.
The combining of QD-modified membranes with other advanced treatment technologies, such as electrochemical oxidation shows its potential for the removal of emerging contaminants, which can be a promising research branch in the membrane separation. Except for the effective application in removal of emerging contaminants, QD-modified membranes also have great potential in gas separation (carbon emission reduction, volatile organic compounds removal, recovery of helium from natural gas), seawater desalination, rare element recovery, etc. However, there are limited researches on the application of QD-modified membranes in these fields. More experimental and theoretical research efforts are expected to be devoted towards these fields. By addressing these challenges, researchers can further advance the development and application of QD-modified membrane materials for industrial wastewater treatment, leading to more efficient and sustainable treatment solutions.
